title: Cat Head Pressing: Causes and Emergency Action Guide
meta_desc: Cat head pressing is a sign of serious neurological distress. Learn why this is a high-urgency emergency and what steps you need to take immediately.
content_html:
What is Head Pressing?

Head pressing is a clinical sign where a cat compulsively pushes their head against a wall, floor, or other firm object for no apparent reason. This is distinctly different from 'head bunting,' the affectionate behavior where a cat rubs their face against you or furniture. Head pressing is typically a sign of severe neurological distress or damage to the central nervous system.

Urgency Level: High

If you observe your cat pressing their head against surfaces, treat this as a high-priority medical emergency. This behavior often indicates that the prosencephalon (the forebrain) is being affected by an underlying condition. Potential causes include toxic poisoning (such as lead or antifreeze), brain tumors, stroke, severe liver disease, or infectious diseases like feline infectious peritonitis (FIP). Immediate veterinary intervention is required to diagnose the cause and begin life-saving treatment.

Why Video and Photos Help Triage

When you call an emergency clinic, having a short video of the behavior can be invaluable. Cats often hide their symptoms or behave differently due to the stress of travel and a clinical environment. A clear video allows the veterinarian to see the exact nature of the head pressing and any accompanying neurological signs, such as dilated pupils or circling, which helps them prioritize your cat's care the moment you walk through the door.

faqs.question: Is head pressing the same as rubbing their head against me?
faqs.answer: No. Affectionate rubbing (bunting) is a social behavior. Head pressing is a compulsive, often stationary act where the cat pushes its head into a hard surface and may seem dazed or unresponsive.

faqs.question: Can I wait until the morning to see my regular vet?
faqs.answer: No. Head pressing is a sign of a life-threatening neurological or metabolic issue. You should take your cat to the nearest 24-hour emergency veterinary hospital immediately.

faqs.question: What other symptoms should I look for?
faqs.answer: Look for additional signs such as pacing, circling, changes in vision, seizures, or a sudden change in personality. All of these reinforce the need for urgent care.
